top_categories.workout_context: 118ft/mi means long efforts at or near FTP on every climb. If your threshold cracks at minute 15, you'll walk the rest.
At Les Trois Ballons, surges push you above threshold and you have to recover while still riding. Over-unders train exactly that.
13,186ft of climbing means repeated surges above threshold. VO2max work is how you survive the fifth climb, not just the first.
At mile 60 of 112, glycogen is gone. This workout teaches your body to produce watts on fumes.
Race-pace efforts that mimic Les Trois Ballons' demands. Pacing, fueling, and tactical decisions under fatigue. Practice the race before June.
88–92% FTP is where you'll spend the hardest sustained miles of Les Trois Ballons. Train it until it feels boring.
13,186ft of climbing on grades that change every minute. You need to switch from seated to standing without losing rhythm.
Les Trois Ballons' mixed terrain forces constant power changes. Surge over the rough stuff, settle on the smooth, repeat for 112 miles.
Every hard session works better with a bigger aerobic base. At 112 miles, base fitness is the difference between racing and surviving.
Endurance with intensity spikes baked in. Exactly what 13,186ft of climbing on mixed terrain does to your power file on race day.
Low-cadence force work for 13,186ft of climbing. The slow, grinding ascents where you can't spin—you push.
Two threshold sessions per week without the recovery cost of VO2max work. For 112 miles, sustainable FTP gains matter more than peak power.

race_overlay.nutrition: Target 60–80g carbs/hour for Les Trois Ballons’s 112 miles. Start fueling within the first 30 minutes — early fueling prevents late-race collapse. Bonking at mile 60 is a nutrition failure, not a fitness failure.
race_overlay.altitude: Moderate altitude at Les Trois Ballons. Arrive 2–3 days early. Reduce intensity expectations by 5–10%. Hydrate aggressively.

pack_summary: This 10-workout pack focuses on TT Threshold, Over Under, and VO2max to prepare you for 112 miles of mixed terrain in Ronchamp, Haute-Saône, France.
